clinical_rationale.summary: The current evidence base for MS brain MRI protocol design emphasizes multiparametric acquisition including T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast imaging with subtraction maps, T2/FLAIR sequences for lesion burden assessment, and T2*-weighted or susceptibility-weighted imaging for cortical and infratentorial lesion detection. At 3T, the signal-to-noise ratio advantage over 1.5T allows thinner slices and higher spatial resolution without proportional scan time penalties, improving detection of small infratentorial and cortical lesions. Subtraction maps from co-registered pre- and post-contrast T1 sequences have been shown to reduce missed contrast-enhancing lesion rate by nearly 80%, making T1 subtraction a standard of care element. Emerging 7T data demonstrate further gains in lesion conspicuity but are not yet the routine clinical standard for MS brain protocols.
clinical_rationale.key_changes: The 2024-2026 literature, particularly from European Radiology (PMID 41468656), establishes T1 subtraction maps as a mandatory adjunct to standard post-contrast T1 imaging in MS, reducing false-negative examinations by approximately 90%. RadioGraphics (PMID 41411163) and Investigative Radiology (PMID 37185832) reinforce the growing role of 7T MRI for superior lesion detection and sodium MRI biomarkers, though these remain research-grade additions rather than routine protocol elements. The Pediatric Radiology abstract (PMID 40095042) at 7T identifies an optimal TE window of 20-30 ms for T2*-weighted sequences to balance contrast and artifact, consistent with established 3T practice. There is no new high-parameter-density evidence from Radiology or JMRI in this abstract set that would override the MAGNIMS/CMSC consensus parameter ranges; therefore, standard protocol values derived from those guidelines and clinical knowledge remain the primary reference, supplemented by the subtraction map evidence.
